topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the blueprint spread across the worn oak table. The schematics for the chronometer were undeniably the same. Every delicate etching, every annotated calculation, mirrored the notes scrawled in his own journal from three weeks prior. A slow, cold pressure began to build behind his eyes. He picked up his pen, his hand trembling slightly as he traced a line on the diagram, his gaze flickering to the empty chair opposite him. It felt as though the room had subtly shifted, the familiar scent of old paper suddenly sharp and alien. He leaned back, rubbing his temples, the edges of his vision blurring into an indistinct wash of grey. "This can't be right," he murmured, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet workshop.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights hummed with an irritating, monotonous drone above the workbench. Clara picked up the prototype casing, turning it over in her hands. The texture, the precise placement of the micro-circuitry—it was an exact replica of the design she had painstakingly finalized last Tuesday. A strange hollowness settled in her chest, a feeling like a vacuum had been created just beneath her ribs. She dropped the casing onto the velvet cloth with a soft thud, her breath catching in her throat. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the soldering station and the window, her movements jerky and uncoordinated. "Who else has access to this level of detail?" she asked the empty air, her voice wavering with an unfamiliar tremor.

[story 3]

Marcus found the final component resting on the assembly tray. It was the last piece of the puzzle, the culmination of weeks of intense, focused labor. As his fingers brushed the cool metal, a wave of profound unease washed over him, making his skin prickle. He looked up, and the sight of the adjacent workstation—identical tools, identical half-finished models—made him freeze mid-reach. His jaw tightened, and he took a ragged, shallow breath. He felt profoundly unmoored, as if the ground beneath his feet had dissolved. "I need a moment," he managed, his tone flat, his eyes darting rapidly between the two identical setups before settling on nothing in particular.
